card.intuition: Find the hyperplane that separates classes with the maximum margin. The margin = 2/||w||. Soft-margin SVM allows slack variables (misclassifications) penalised by C. The decision boundary is wᵀx + b = 0; hinge loss = max(0, 1 - y·(wᵀx+b)).
card.analogy: Drawing the widest possible road between two groups of people. A narrow road works but a wide one gives more safety margin. Support vectors are the people standing closest to the road's edges — they define the road width.
card.time_complexity: O(n · d) per SGD step; O(n² · d) for kernel SVM with SMO
card.space_complexity: O(d) for weights (linear SVM); O(n_sv) for kernel SVM (support vectors)
when_to_use: High-dimensional data (text, gene expression) where a linear boundary often works well
When maximum margin is desirable for robust generalisation
As a baseline before trying kernel SVM or neural networks
gotchas: C controls the margin-accuracy tradeoff: large C = fewer misclassifications (narrow margin), small C = wide margin (more misclassifications allowed)
Scale features before SVM — the margin in pixel space ≠ in normalised space
SVM predicts labels, not probabilities — use Platt scaling to get probabilities

exercise.prompt: Implement linear SVM training using sub-gradient SGD on the hinge loss.
hints: When inside the margin (y*score < 1), the sub-gradient is -C*y*x. Update: w = w*(1-eta) + eta*C*y[i]*X[i][j]
exercise.test_code:
# Linearly separable: y=+1 if x0>x1, y=-1 otherwise
X = [[2,0],[3,1],[4,0],[0,2],[1,3],[0,4]]
y = [1, 1, 1, -1, -1, -1]
w = linear_svm_sgd(X, y, C=1.0, lr=0.1, epochs=200)
preds = predict_svm(X, w)
assert preds == y, f'Got {preds}'
print('PASS')
